FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes 9855 children from the U.S. ABCD dataset (ABCD release 3.0), ages 9-10 at baseline, 21 recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent change score models (SEM) with baseline and 2-year follow-up ABCD data, reporting path coefficients for screen time types on change in intelligence.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 2 qualifies: the 'Socializing' screen time category is explicitly constructed from exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items — texting (item 4: text on a cell phone, tablet, or computer), social networking (item 5: visit social networking sites e.g. Facebook, Twitter, Instagram), and video chat (item 6: use video chat e.g. Skype, Facetime). Coefficients for Socializing paired with intelligence (individual-trait cognitive outcome) are reported throughout (e.g., baseline r = -0.10; longitudinal beta = -0.07 non-significant; path coefficients in Fig. 1). The other side (intelligence) is an individual-trait-level cognitive measure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: Pearson r at baseline (Socializing r = -0.10, p<0.001; Watching r = -0.12, p<0.001), standardized path coefficients from latent change score models (e.g., Socializing beta = -0.07, p=0.220 longitudinal; Watching beta = +0.12, p=0.047), and gender-stratified chi-square difference tests.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed: confirmatory factor analysis, latent change score SEM models, gender multigroup analyses, sibling analyses — all reporting numeric estimates for screen time type associations with intelligence.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (Socializing, Watching, Gaming) is used as IV predicting intelligence (DV) throughout the paper; design is longitudinal with SM/phone as IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Socializing composite (texting + social networking + video chat) is exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing and qualifies under Step 2. Gaming includes all device types (Xbox, PlayStation, iPad) per methods — gaming alone would not qualify but Socializing independently meets IC2. The paper includes cross-sectional baseline correlations and longitudinal change-score models.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD cohort, 11,000+ children aged 9-11 at baseline, 21 nationally distributed sites, ABCD Data Release 3.0 (https://nda.nih.gov/study.html?id=901).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear regression, cross-lagged panel models (CLPM), and serial mediation analyses on ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in EBioMedicine; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found. The only screen time variable is STU (screen time utilization), a single composite aggregating time across all electronic devices. Methods state STU was 'the total amount of self-reported time using various electronic devices on both typical weekdays and weekend days' (abcd_ssmty01), including spending time on Facebook AND watching movies — mixing SM with TV and other non-phone activities. No per-device or per-platform breakout with its own coefficient is reported anywhere (main results, Table 2, or figures). Step 2: The STU composite bundles smartphone/tablet time with television and computer time; no separate phone/SM-only breakout exists. This fails under the multi-device aggregated time rule (composite includes TV and unspecified device time without separate breakout).

notes: STU is defined as total screen time across all devices (tablets, television, computers, smartphones) with no per-device breakdown in any analysis. Even though some items include SM (Facebook), the composite is reported as a single undifferentiated aggregate throughout all analyses. Fails IC2 Step 2 under multi-device aggregation rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study participants with useable baseline structural imaging (N=10,691), baseline data from ABCD Release 3.0, 21 sites, ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: JIVE dimension reduction followed by generalized linear mixed models (GLMMs) examining associations between structural covariation patterns and SMA/psychopathology/cognition/sleep.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions; no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found anywhere in the paper. The only screen time variable is total SMA hours — a composite of 6 items: (1) watching TV shows/movies, (2) watching videos, (3) playing video games, (4) texting, (5) visiting social networking sites, and (6) video-chatting. No per-modality breakout with its own coefficient is reported in main results, supplementary, sensitivity, or appendix (only Appendix Table A1 reports demographic predictors of total SMA, still the aggregate). Step 2: The SMA composite includes TV watching (item 1) and video games (item 3) — non-phone/non-SM ingredients — alongside texting/SM/video-chat, without any separate breakout. Fails Step 2 under the composite-content rule.

notes: SMA composite explicitly includes TV shows/movies, video games, texting, social networking, and video chat — a mixed bundle. No analysis isolates texting, social media, or video chat with its own coefficient. The paper's only screen time predictor/outcome is the aggregate total SMA hours. Excluded under FT-EC2.
